records.initiation: 100,000 valid signatures within 18 months for a constitutional initiative; 50,000 within 100 days or eight cantons for an optional referendum
records.vote_rule: simple popular majority for optional referendums; popular majority plus canton majority for constitutional change
records.institutional_bridge: Federal Council and Parliament may make counterproposals; referendum-capable groups influence legislative drafting
records.observed_signal: As of 2025, 237 federal popular initiatives had reached a vote and 26 were accepted; 219 optional referendums had been held and voters rejected 91 proposals
failure_or_equity_questions: canton majority gives territorial units unequal per-person weight
frequent voting can produce participation and information burdens
initiative text operates at constitutional level
records.us_transfer_limit: Swiss cantons, party bargaining, scale, and constitutional structure are not equivalent to U.S. states or Article V

records.initiation: Constitutional reform may be initiated by 10 percent of registered citizens; rejective referendum and legislative initiative rules use much higher electorate thresholds
records.vote_rule: constitutional proposals go to the electorate under constitutional approval rules
records.binding_status: binding when constitutional requirements are met
records.institutional_bridge: The General Assembly can consider and, in specified routes, place alternatives before voters
records.observed_signal: National constitutional questions have been placed alongside general elections, including pension and search-authority proposals in 2024
failure_or_equity_questions: high signature thresholds favor organized national coalitions
complex fiscal rules can be constitutionalized
simultaneous election context can nationalize unrelated questions
records.us_transfer_limit: Compulsory voting, a unitary national electorate, and Uruguay's amendment procedures differ fundamentally from the U.S.

records.initiation: The Oireachtas establishes an assembly and terms of reference; constitutional change proceeds through the representative process and referendum
records.vote_rule: Assembly recommendations are voted by members but remain advisory; voters decide constitutional amendments
records.binding_status: assembly advisory; referendum binding for constitutional change
records.institutional_bridge: A parliamentary committee and government publicly respond to assembly recommendations
records.observed_signal: Assemblies use broadly representative random selection, expert and stakeholder evidence, facilitated deliberation, published votes, and public reports
failure_or_equity_questions: agenda remains institutionally controlled
recommendations can be delayed or declined
recruitment quality and attrition can affect legitimacy
records.us_transfer_limit: Ireland is smaller and unitary; its assembly model informs deliberation more directly than U.S. constitutional authority

records.initiation: 500,000 voters or five Regional Councils may request total or partial repeal of a law or law-equivalent act
records.vote_rule: a majority of eligible voters must participate and a majority of valid votes must approve
records.institutional_bridge: Legal admissibility and statutory implementation constrain the ballot
records.observed_signal: The constitutional design provides a citizen veto rather than a general citizen power to enact statutes
failure_or_equity_questions: turnout quorum makes abstention a strategic no vote
exclusions remove core fiscal decisions
partial repeal can be difficult to explain
records.us_transfer_limit: The mechanism is most relevant to repeal design, not affirmative national citizen legislation or annual program renewal

records.initiation: A citizen policy proposal within Executive Yuan jurisdiction becomes a response case after 5,000 verified supports within 60 days
records.vote_rule: support threshold triggers consideration and response, not enactment
records.binding_status: agenda and response right; nonbinding on policy outcome
records.institutional_bridge: Responsible authorities contact the proposer, assess feasibility, may convene collaborative meetings, and publish a reasoned response within two months, extendable once
records.observed_signal: Proposals, support, comments, responses, and status are retained on a public platform
failure_or_equity_questions: digital access and organized mobilization affect agenda access
agency jurisdiction limits eligible proposals
a response duty does not guarantee adoption
records.us_transfer_limit: Best compared with a federal petition-and-response layer, not a binding citizen chamber

records.initiation: At least one million valid statements of support, including minimum thresholds in at least seven member states
records.vote_rule: qualifying support triggers Commission examination and response rather than a popular vote
records.binding_status: agenda and response right; nonbinding on legislation
records.institutional_bridge: The European Commission evaluates the initiative within its legal competence and explains intended action or nonaction
records.observed_signal: The geographic threshold explicitly requires cross-member-state organization
failure_or_equity_questions: high transnational coordination costs
Commission agenda authority remains decisive
signature success can be mistaken for legislative adoption
records.us_transfer_limit: Offers a geographic-support gate but not a model of binding state-weighted enactment

records.initiation: Rules vary; 24 states, the District of Columbia, and the U.S. Virgin Islands have an initiative process, generally using petition review, titles, signature collection, and verification
records.vote_rule: usually a majority, with state-specific higher thresholds, subject rules, geographic requirements, and conflicting-measure rules
records.binding_status: generally binding under state law, with varying legislative amendment restrictions
records.institutional_bridge: Indirect initiatives first go to the legislature, which may enact the proposal or sometimes offer a competing measure
records.observed_signal: No two state qualification systems are identical; single-subject, fiscal, title, signature, and geographic rules vary substantially
failure_or_equity_questions: paid signature and campaign markets advantage wealth
ballot wording and complexity affect understanding
constitutional initiatives can lock detailed policy into higher law
geographic requirements can become legally or operationally defective
records.us_transfer_limit: State experience is directly relevant operationally but does not create federal initiative authority

first_depth_priorities.reason: binding initiative, referendum, counterproposal, and true popular-plus-canton double majority
deliberative evidence layer before institutional and referendum action
closest operational, legal, campaign, and administration evidence for American transfer

transfer_hypothesis: The strongest initial U.S. design is likely a hybrid: Irish-style deliberative preparation, U.S.-state-style qualification and administration safeguards, and a separately tested Swiss-inspired double majority for only the highest-impact questions.
